title: Modulo Calculator - Remainder of a mod b
shortDescription: Calculate a mod b, the remainder after dividing a by b.
description: A free online modulo calculator that computes a mod b, the remainder left after dividing a by b. Enter a dividend and a non-zero divisor to get the result instantly.
metaDescription: Free modulo calculator to compute a mod b, the remainder after division. Enter dividend a and divisor b for an instant, accurate result for math and programming.

result.label: Remainder (a mod b)
result.empty: Enter a value for a and a non-zero value for b.

sections.heading: What is the modulo operation?
paragraphs: The modulo operation, written a mod b, returns the remainder after dividing a by b. For example, 17 mod 5 equals 2, because 17 divided by 5 is 3 with 2 left over.
The divisor b must not be zero, since division by zero is undefined.

sections.heading: Where modulo is used
paragraphs: Modulo is everywhere in math and programming: checking even or odd numbers, wrapping array indices, building hash tables, and clock arithmetic.
It is also the foundation of modular arithmetic used in cryptography and number theory.

howTo.name: Enter the dividend
howTo.text: Type the value of a, the number you want to divide.

howTo.name: Enter the divisor
howTo.text: Type the value of b, which must not be zero.

howTo.name: Read the result
howTo.text: The remainder of a mod b appears automatically.

faq.q: What does a mod b mean?
faq.a: It is the remainder left after dividing a by b. For example, 17 mod 5 equals 2.
Division by zero is undefined in mathematics, so the divisor b must be a non-zero number.

faq.q: How is modulo of negative numbers handled?
faq.a: Results follow standard math conventions; the sign of the remainder may differ from some programming languages.

faq.q: Is the result always smaller than b?
faq.a: For positive operands, the remainder is always between 0 and b minus 1.

faq.q: What is modulo used for in programming?
faq.a: Common uses include checking even or odd numbers, cycling through indices, and hashing.
